metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the allowed link-target set in the Testing section and AC4 so both list the same exhaustive set (e.g., `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` — or explicitly drop any that are unintended).
evidence: Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'
AC4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)' — omits `http://` and includes `mailto:` inconsistently
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allowed-target set is specified differently in Testing vs AC: Testing lists `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, and `mailto:` as allowed; AC4 lists only `https://` and `#anchor`. An implementer writing the guard test against the Testing spec will pass `http://` and `mailto:` links that AC4 would reject — or vice versa.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) vs AC item 4
scenarios: Implementer writes the guard to allow `mailto:` (Testing) but the AC verifier rejects it, or vice versa for `http://`.
A future guide added with an `http://` link passes the test but someone reading the AC flags it as non-compliant.
findings.suggested_fix: Standardise to: `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` in both Testing and AC4, since `mailto:` and `http://` are legitimate in real prose and the current omissions appear accidental.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the link-resolution guard's allowed-target set between the Testing section and AC4: decide whether `http://` links are allowed (Testing says yes; AC4 says no) and state the rule once consistently in both places.
evidence: Testing: 'allowed iff https:// / http:// (off-repo), an intra-doc #anchor, or mailto:'
AC4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only https:// / #anchor / mailto:)'
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allowed-target set is specified inconsistently between the Testing section and AC4: Testing lists `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, and `mailto:` as allowed; AC4 lists only `https://`, `#anchor`, and `mailto:` — omitting `http://`. An implementer reading AC4 will write a guard that rejects `http://` links (e.g. any legacy HTTP link in a guide), while an implementer reading Testing will allow them. One of these is wrong for the intended semantics.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: Implementer writes guard from AC4: rejects `http://` links in guide bodies, causing false failures if any existing guide reference uses HTTP.
Implementer writes guard from Testing: allows `http://` links, potentially permitting insecure or redirect-only links that the AC intends to block.
findings.suggested_fix: Since packaged guides should only link to well-known hosted URLs (all HTTPS on GitHub), the stricter AC4 rule (`https://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` only) is more correct — update the Testing paragraph to match: replace 'https:// / http:// (off-repo)' with 'https:// (off-repo)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the link-resolution guard's allowed-target set: pick ONE canonical list (Testing vs. AC4 disagree on whether `http://` is a valid target) and apply it consistently in both sections so the test implementor has a single unambiguous rule to code against.
evidence: Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'
AC4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)'
`http://` appears in Testing's allow-list but is absent from AC4's allow-list — two specifications of the same guard rule differ on this value
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allowed-target vocabulary — a decision rule the new test must implement — is defined inconsistently between the Testing section and AC4: Testing permits `https://`, `http://`, `#anchor`, and `mailto:` as valid targets; AC4 permits only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:` (silently drops `http://`). An implementor reading the plan cannot determine whether `http://` links in packaged guide prose should PASS or FAIL the guard.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: A packaged guide contains an `http://` link (e.g. to a legacy non-TLS URL). The Testing spec says PASS; the AC spec says FAIL. The test author must choose, and the choice is unspecified.
A future guide author reads AC4 as the authoritative spec and avoids `http://` links, not knowing Testing intended to allow them.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is HTTPS-only (consistent with `_DEFAULT_DOCS_URL` using `https://`), remove `http://` from the Testing paragraph. If plain-HTTP links in guides are intentionally permitted, add `http://` to AC4's parenthetical. Either way, a single canonical allow-list should appear in one place and be cited by the other.

findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allowed-form set is defined inconsistently across three plan sections, leaving the implementer with genuine ambiguity about which set to code the test against. Approach says `https://`/`http://` (off-repo) plus `#anchor`; Testing says `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), `#anchor`, and `mailto:`; AC #4 says only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:` — omitting `http://`. Additionally, the Testing section's prose-mention sub-check ('validates every `rebar explain <topic>` cited in prose names a real registered guide/criterion') has no corresponding AC item, so an implementer can satisfy all ACs while omitting that half of the guard.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) and AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one canonical allow-list (suggest: `https://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` for markdown link targets — no `http://` since all canonical links should be HTTPS) and state it once in Testing; promote the prose `rebar explain <topic>` validation to its own AC bullet (e.g., 'every `rebar explain <topic>` cited in any guide body names a real registered guide or criterion id (test passes)').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'verified by reading the rendered guides' in AC5 with a reference to the AC4 guard test as the primary proving command (e.g. 'confirmed by the link-resolution guard test passing') so AC5 has a deterministic, repeatable prover.
evidence: AC5: 'rebar explain review and rebar explain plan no longer reference repo-relative files; sibling guides read as rebar explain <topic>, repo docs as https://github.com/navapbc/… (verified by reading the rendered guides)'
'verified by reading the rendered guides' is a manual step, not a deterministic command
The link-resolution guard test in AC4 would catch any remaining repo-relative link targets automatically, but AC5 doesn't reference it
findings.finding: AC5's proving command is 'verified by reading the rendered guides' — a manual, subjective check rather than a deterministic command. While the link-resolution guard test from AC4 mechanically covers the same claim, AC5 does not invoke it as its prover, leaving the AC without a concrete, repeatable proving command of its own.
scenarios: An executor completes the file edits and the guard test passes (AC4), but AC5 is marked complete based on a quick manual read that misses a dangling reference — or conversely, an executor spends time doing a manual read when the guard test already provides machine-verified evidence
In a CI-only environment (no human reviewer), AC5 has no automated proving command
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC5 to: '`rebar explain review` and `rebar explain plan` no longer reference repo-relative files — confirmed by the link-resolution guard test (AC4) passing; an executor may additionally run `rebar explain review` and `rebar explain plan` to spot-check prose readability.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the link-resolution allow-list between the Testing section and AC4: explicitly state whether bare `http://` links (in addition to `https://`) are permitted in packaged guides, and use the same list in both places.
evidence: Testing: 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`'

AC4: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)' — actually AC4 DOES include mailto, re-reading: 'only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`' — wait, let me re-read AC4 carefully
AC4 text: 'no markdown link target is consumer-relative (only `https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`)'

But the Testing section says 'allowed iff `https://`/`http://` (off-repo), an intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:`' — Testing drops `http://` as a separate pattern but names it; AC4 says '`https://`/`#anchor`/`mailto:`' omitting bare `http://`

The real discrepancy is that Testing explicitly lists `http://` separately from `https://` while AC4 only lists `https://` — an executor might write a guard that rejects `http://` links (which may or may not appear in guides)
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard's allow-list is specified inconsistently: the Testing section lists `https://`/`http://`, `#anchor`, and `mailto:` as allowed targets, but AC4 lists only `https://`/`#anchor` — omitting `mailto:`. An executor implementing the guard test has two conflicting specs and must guess whether `mailto:` links in packaged guides should pass or fail.
findings.location: Testing section (link-resolution guard paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: An executor writes the guard to reject bare `http://` links, then a guide uses one (e.g. an http redirect URL), causing a spurious failure
Alternatively the executor allows `http://` per the Testing section but not per AC4, creating a divergence between the test spec and the AC
findings.suggested_fix: In both the Testing paragraph and AC4, use the identical list: `https://` (and optionally `http://`), `#anchor`, `mailto:` — pick one and state it once, then reference it from the other location.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` has been updated to name the CLI discovery paths (`rebar explain commit-trailer` / `rebar verify-commit-ticket --help`).
evidence: Scope: '`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` — note the CLI discovery paths.'
AC section: no AC covers this file or any verification of its updated content.
findings.finding: `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` is listed as a Scope deliverable with described work ("note the CLI discovery paths") but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, making it unverifiable and potentially forgotten at review time.
findings.location: Scope section — `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` bullet; Acceptance Criteria section (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: Implementer skips the docs/ update since no AC will fail; reviewer has no criterion to check the file was updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` mentions `rebar explain commit-trailer` and `rebar verify-commit-ticket --help` as discovery paths (grep/read the file).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the packaged-guide link convention (only `https://`, `#anchor`, `mailto:` targets; sibling guides via `rebar explain <topic>`) in a contributor-visible location — a short note in AGENTS.md or a comment at the top of `src/rebar/_guides/` (e.g., a `README.md` in that directory) — so future guide authors encounter the rule before CI does.
evidence: Plan says AGENTS.md update is only 'one-line pointer to rebar explain commit-trailer in the trailer bullet' — no mention of documenting the link-format convention for guide authors
Guard test description: 'asserts each target RESOLVES for a non-rebar client: allowed iff https:///http:// (off-repo), an intra-doc #anchor, or mailto:' — convention lives only in test code
Scope section lists no ADR or AGENTS.md section for the link-convention rule
T5e 'novel architectural decision is captured in ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc' — the guide-authoring link rule qualifies as such a decision
findings.finding: The new architectural convention that packaged `_guides/*.md` files may only contain `https://`, intra-doc `#anchor`, or `mailto:` links (no consumer-relative paths) is enforced by a guard test but is not documented in any contributor-visible authoring guide (AGENTS.md, CONTRIBUTING, or the new guide itself). A contributor adding a future packaged guide will only discover the constraint at CI-fail time rather than during authoring.
findings.location: Approach section / Testing section (link-resolution guard); AGENTS.md scope item
scenarios: A developer adds a fourth packaged guide and writes a relative-path link to a sibling guide (e.g., `](passing-code-review.md)`), ships it, and only finds out it violates policy when the guard test fails in CI — wasting a round-trip that a one-line author note would have prevented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `src/rebar/_guides/README.md` (or a comment block at the top of each guide) stating: 'Links in packaged guides must resolve for repo-less installs: use https:// for external docs, `rebar explain <topic>` for sibling guides, and #anchor for intra-doc links. Relative file paths are forbidden and enforced by the link-resolution guard test.' Alternatively, add one bullet to AGENTS.md under a 'Authoring packaged guides' heading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Testing section whether the link-resolution guard's `](target)` regex is intended to be the only detection method, or whether it should also scan for parenthetical bare-path patterns like `(docs/…)` and `(../…)` to cover non-hyperlink references such as the one at passing-code-review.md L35.
evidence: Guard spec: 'extracts every markdown link `](target)`' — regex pattern `](target)` only matches `[link text](target)` syntax
passing-code-review.md L35: 'claimed ticket (`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md`)' — parenthetical, not a hyperlink
passing-code-review.md L39: 'see CONTRIBUTING.md §1b / AGENTS.md' — plain prose, not a hyperlink
The plan does correctly scope fixing L35 and L39, but the guard as specified won't enforce the fix or prevent recurrence
findings.finding: The link-resolution guard is specced to extract markdown links using the `](target)` pattern, but the most important dangling reference in `passing-code-review.md` is at L35: `claimed ticket (\`docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md\`)` — this is a parenthetical/inline-code reference, NOT a markdown hyperlink `[text](url)`, so it will NOT be caught by the `](target)` regex after the plan's fix is applied. Similarly, L39's `(see CONTRIBUTING.md §1b / AGENTS.md)` is plain prose text. The plan does enumerate fixing these specific lines, but the guard won't prevent future regressions in this reference style.
scenarios: After this plan lands, a future edit re-introduces a parenthetical bare path like `(docs/foo.md)` in a packaged guide — the link-resolution guard silently passes it because the regex only matches `](…)` syntax.
The plan fixes L35 in execution, but the guard test won't verify that fix was actually made (it uses a different detection pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide whether to migrate the existing `test_expected_format_documented_in_reference` drift test (currently pinning `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` via a fragile `parents[2]` path) to also pin the new packaged guide, or add a `pytest.importorskip`/`pytest.mark.skipif` guard so it gracefully skips when the `docs/` tree is absent.
evidence: Existing test: `doc = Path(rebar.__file__).parents[2] / 'docs' / 'commit-ticket-trailer.md'`
Plan scope: 'tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py — drift assertion (packaged guide quotes EXPECTED_FORMAT)'
Plan does not mention updating the existing docs-relative drift test
findings.finding: The plan adds a new drift assertion for the packaged guide (in `test_verify_commit_ticket.py`) but does not reconcile the existing drift test at line 233-236 of that same file, which pins `docs/commit-ticket-trailer.md` via a repo-relative `Path(rebar.__file__).parents[2] / 'docs' / 'commit-ticket-trailer.md'` path. This path is unreachable in an installed (repo-less) environment — the exact failure mode the plan is fixing. As written, a repo-less CI run will pass the new packaged-guide drift test but potentially fail or skip the old docs-relative test, leaving a latent fragility that contradicts the plan's goal.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py:233-236 (existing test) vs. plan's new drift assertion in the same file
scenarios: An installed rebar (no docs/ tree) runs the test suite: the new packaged-guide drift test passes; the existing docs-relative test raises FileNotFoundError or silently skips, leaving the drift guarantee incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/mcp-reference.md` to the Scope and note that `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` must be re-run after updating the `explain_criterion` docstring in `_mcp_reads.py` (the CI drift gate in `test_gen_mcp_reference.py:test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` will otherwise fail).
evidence: Plan Scope: 'src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py — the MCP explain_criterion tool docstring hardcodes the guide list (plan/review); add commit-trailer so MCP clients discover it'
gen_mcp_reference.py renders docs/mcp-reference.md from the actual docstrings; test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree asserts gen.main(['--check']) == 0
docs/mcp-reference.md currently shows explain_criterion summary line from its docstring; changing the docstring will change the generated output
docs/mcp-reference.md absent from plan Scope
findings.finding: The `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` generator produces `docs/mcp-reference.md` from MCP tool docstrings at build time; a CI drift gate (`test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` in `tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py`) fails the build if the committed file is stale. When the plan updates the `explain_criterion` docstring in `_mcp_reads.py` to name `commit-trailer`, the generated reference will change, but `docs/mcp-reference.md` is not listed in the plan's Scope and the regeneration step is not enumerated.
scenarios: CI runs test_gen_mcp_reference.py after the docstring change; test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree exits non-zero because docs/mcp-reference.md no longer matches the generated output — `make test` fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: `docs/mcp-reference.md — regenerate via \`python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py\` after the docstring change (the CI drift gate asserts this file matches the generator output).`

findings.finding: The MCP tool docstring in `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` (lines 60-61) hardcodes the guide list as '``plan`` = how to write a passing plan; ``review`` = how to pass code review' — adding `commit-trailer` to `AUTHOR_GUIDES` is not reflected there, and `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` is absent from the plan's Scope section. The plan asserts the MCP `explain_guide` tool will serve the new guide, but the docstring visible to MCP clients will silently omit `commit-trailer`.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_mcp_reads.py`, change the docstring to derive the guide list dynamically (e.g. reference `AUTHOR_GUIDES` keys) or update the static string to include `'commit-trailer' = how to format the commit-ticket trailer`.

findings.finding: The MCP `explain_criterion` tool docstring hardcodes the valid guide-name vocabulary (`"plan" = …; "review" = …`) — the instruction an MCP client/LLM reads to discover which `criterion_id` values name guides. After `commit-trailer` is added to `AUTHOR_GUIDES`, this docstring is stale and an MCP consumer following only the tool schema will never discover the new topic. The plan's Scope lists `registry.py`, guide files, and test files but never mentions `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py`.
findings.location: src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py lines 57-63 (explain_criterion tool docstring); Plan § Scope (omits _mcp_reads.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py`, replace the hardcoded `("plan" = …; "review" = …)` in the `explain_criterion` docstring with a dynamically-constructed sentence, e.g. build it at registration time from `registry.AUTHOR_GUIDES`, so adding a new guide to `AUTHOR_GUIDES` automatically updates the tool description without a separate file change.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
